course.title: Phonora
course.summary: A guided path from phonetic symbols to reading rules.
course.description: Phonora helps learners recognise phonetic symbols, hear sound contrasts, and connect transcription with reading rules.
course.hero_headline: Learn to read English pronunciation without guessing.
course.hero_subheadline: Move from symbols to combinations to reading rules with audio, examples, and focused exercises.

modules.title: Individual phonetic symbols
modules.summary: Meet the core vowel and consonant symbols, hear them clearly, and compare them with nearby sounds.

modules.title: Sound combinations and diphthongs
modules.summary: Study how sounds combine and how those combinations differ from similar patterns.

modules.title: Reading rules
modules.summary: Connect spelling patterns, transcription, and pronunciation through reusable examples.

modules.title: Practice lab
modules.summary: Use mixed exercises to reinforce symbols, audio recognition, stress, and rule application.

exampleWords.note: The final e changes the vowel sound.
Long vowel produced by the final e pattern.
No final e, so the vowel stays short.

phoneticSymbols.title: Short i sound
phoneticSymbols.explanation: The /ɪ/ sound is short, relaxed, and common in words such as ship and sit.
phoneticSymbols.stress_note: In unstressed syllables it may weaken further, so learners often over-pronounce it.
phoneticSymbols.common_mistakes: Learners often confuse /ɪ/ with /iː/ and make it too long.
phoneticSymbols.comparison_note: Compare ship /ʃɪp/ with sheep /ʃiːp/ to hear length and tongue position differences.

soundCombinations.title: Diphthong /eɪ/
soundCombinations.explanation: This diphthong begins near /e/ and glides toward /ɪ/, as in day and cake.
soundCombinations.stress_note: In stressed syllables the glide is clearer; rushed speech can shorten it.
soundCombinations.common_mistakes: Learners may replace the glide with a flat /e/ sound.
soundCombinations.comparison_note: Compare /eɪ/ in day with /æ/ in dad to hear the glide clearly.

readingRules.title: Final e changes the vowel
readingRules.rule_statement: When a word ends with silent e, the earlier vowel often says its name.
readingRules.explanation: In many one-syllable words, a final silent e changes a short vowel into a long vowel or diphthong pattern.
readingRules.exceptions: This pattern is common but not universal; borrowed words and irregular spellings may break it.
readingRules.limitations: Treat it as a useful pattern, not a perfect law. Always confirm with transcription when possible.
readingRules.practice_intro: Try the linked exercise to decide when final e changes the vowel sound.

exercises.title: MVP phonetics drill
exercises.summary: A mixed practice set covering audio recognition, transcription, stress, and reading rules.
exercises.instructions: Complete each item and use the explanations to compare sounds, stress, and spelling patterns.

items.prompt: Which symbol matches the sound you hear?
items.explanation: The recording targets the short /ɪ/ sound.

items.prompt: Which audio option matches the symbol below?
items.explanation: The /eɪ/ diphthong glides toward /ɪ/, represented by audio option A.

items.prompt: Choose the word that contains the target sound.
Choose the item with stress on the second syllable.
items.explanation: reCORD places the main stress on the second syllable.
Ship contains /ɪ/; sheep and seat use a longer vowel.

items.prompt: Which word shows the final-e reading pattern?
items.explanation: The final e in cape changes the vowel sound compared with cap.

lessonBlocks.title: Meet /ɪ/
lessonBlocks.description: Study the short i sound and hear it in common words.

lessonBlocks.title: Hear /eɪ/ as a diphthong
lessonBlocks.description: Learn how the /eɪ/ glide behaves in words such as day and cake.

lessonBlocks.title: Use final e to predict vowel sound
lessonBlocks.description: Connect the silent-e pattern to transcription and pronunciation.

lessonBlocks.title: Mixed listening and reading drill
lessonBlocks.description: Review symbols, combinations, stress, and reading rules in one exercise lesson.
